title: - PEA-KD: PARAMETER EFFICIENT AND ACCURATE KNOWLEDGE DISTILLATION ON BERT [PDF]

paragraphs.text:
Under review as a conference paper at ICLR 2021

## - PEA-KD: PARAMETER EFFICIENT AND ACCURATE KNOWLEDGE DISTILLATION ON BERT

## **Anonymous authors**

Paper under double-blind review

## ABSTRACT

How can we efficiently compress a model while maintaining its performance? Knowledge Distillation (KD) is one of the widely known methods for model compression. In essence, KD trains a smaller student model based on a larger teacher model and tries to retain the teacher model’s level of performance as much as possible. However, existing KD methods suffer from the following limitations. First, since the student model is smaller in absolute size, it inherently lacks model capacity. Second, the absence of an initial guide for the student model makes it difficult for the student to imitate the teacher model to its fullest. Conventional KD methods yield low performance due to these limitations.

In this paper, we propose Pea-KD (Parameter-efficient and accurate Knowledge Distillation), a novel approach to KD. Pea-KD consists of two main parts: Shuffled Parameter Sharing (SPS) and Pretraining with Teacher’s Predictions (PTP). Using this combination, we are capable of alleviating the KD’s limitations. SPS is a new parameter sharing method that increases the student model capacity. PTP is a KD-specialized initialization method, which can act as a good initial guide for the student. When combined, this method yields a significant increase in student model’s performance. Experiments conducted on BERT with different datasets and tasks show that the proposed approach improves the student model’s performance by 4.4% on average in four GLUE tasks, outperforming existing KD baselines by significant margins.

## 1 INTRODUCTION

_How can we improve the accuracy of knowledge distillation (KD) with smaller number of parameters?_ KD uses a well-trained large teacher model to train a smaller student model. Conventional KD method (Hinton et al. (2006)) trains the student model using the teacher model’s predictions as targets. That is, the student model uses not only the true labels (hard distribution) but also the teacher model’s predictions (soft distribution) as targets. Since better KD accuracy is directly linked to better model compression, improving KD accuracy is valuable and crucial.

Naturally, there have been many studies and attempts to improve the accuracy of KD. Sun et al. (2019) introduced Patient KD, which utilizes not only the teacher model’s final output but also the intermediate outputs generated from the teacher’s layers. Jiao et al. (2019) applied additional KD in the pretraining step of the student model. However, existing KD methods share the limitation of students having lower model capacity compared to their teacher models, due to their smaller size. In addition, there is no proper initialization guide established for the student models, which especially becomes important when the student model is small. These limitations lower than desired levels of student model accuracy.

In this paper, we propose Pea-KD (Parameter-efficient and accurate Knowledge Distillation), a novel KD method designed especially for Transformer-based models (Vaswani et al. (2017)) that significantly improves the student model’s accuracy. Pea-KD is composed of two modules, Shuffled Parameter Sharing (SPS) and Pretraining with Teacher’s Predictions (PTP). Pea-KD is based on the following two main ideas.
1. We apply SPS in order to increase the effective model capacity of the student model without increasing the number of parameters. SPS has two steps: 1) stacking layers that share

1

Under review as a conference paper at ICLR 2021

parameters and 2) shuffling the parameters between shared pairs of layers. Doing so increases the model’s effective capacity which enables the student to better replicate the teacher model (details in Section 3.2).

2. We apply a pretraining task called PTP for the student. Through PTP, the student model learns general knowledge about the teacher and the task. With this additional pretraining, the student more efficiently acquires and utilizes the teacher’s knowledge during the actual KD process (details in Section 3.3).
Throughout the paper we use Pea-KD applied on BERT model (PeaBERT) as an example to investigate our proposed approach. We summarize our main contributions as follows:

- **Novel framework for KD.** We propose SPS and PTP, a parameter sharing method and a KD-specialized initialization method. These methods serve as a new framework for KD to significantly improve accuracy.

- **Performance.** When tested on four widely used GLUE tasks, PeaBERT improves student’s accuracy by 4.4% on average and up to 14.8% maximum when compared to the original BERT model. PeaBERT also outperforms the existing state-of-the-art KD baselines by 3.5% on average.

- **Generality.** Our proposed method Pea-KD can be applied to any transformer-based models

Then we conclude in Section 5.

## 2 RELATED WORK

**Pretrained Language Models.** The framework of first pretraining language models and then finetuning for downstream tasks has now become the industry standard for Natural Language Processing (NLP) models. Pretrained language models, such as BERT (Devlin et al. (2018)), XLNet (Yang et al. (2019)), RoBERTa (Liu et al. (2019)) and ELMo (Peters et al. (2018)) prove how powerful pretrained language models can be. Specifically, BERT is a language model consisting of multiple transformer encoder layers. Transformers (Vaswani et al. (2017)) can capture long-term dependencies between input tokens by using a self-attention mechanism. Self-attention calculates an attention function using three components, query, key, and value, each denoted as matrices Q, K, and V. The attention

It is known that through pretraining using Masked Language Modeling (MLM) and Next Sentence Prediction (NSP), the attention matrices in BERT can capture substantial linguistic knowledge. BERT has achieved the state-of-the-art performance on a wide range of NLP tasks, such as the GLUE benchmark (Wang et al. (2018)) and SQuAD (Rajpurkar et al. (2016)).
However, these modern pretrained models are very large in size and contain millions of parameters, making them nearly impossible to apply on edge devices with limited amount of resources.

**Model Compression.** As deep learning algorithms started getting adopted, implemented, and researched in diverse fields, high computation costs and memory shortage have started to become challenging factors. Especially in NLP, pretrained language models typically require a large set of parameters. This results in extensive cost of computation and memory. As such, Model Compression has now become an important task for deep learning. There have already been many attempts to tackle this problem, including quantization (Gong et al. (2014)) and weight pruning (Han et al. (2015)). Two promising approaches are KD (Hinton et al. (2015)) and Parameter Sharing, which we focus on in this paper.

**Knowledge Distillation (KD).** As briefly covered in Section 1, KD transfers knowledge from a well-trained and large teacher model to a smaller student model. KD uses the teacher models predictions on top of the true labels to train the student model. It is proven through many experiments

2

Under review as a conference paper at ICLR 2021

that the student model learns to imitate the soft distribution of the teacher model’s predictions and ultimately performs better than learning solely from the original data. There have already been many attempts to compress BERT using KD. Patient Knowledge Distillation (Sun et al. (2019)) extracts knowledge not only from the final prediction of the teacher, but also from the intermediate layers. TinyBERT (Jiao et al. (2019)) uses a two-stage learning framework and applies KD in both pretraining and task-specific finetuning. DistilBERT (Sanh et al. (2019)) uses half of the layers of BERT-base model and applies KD during pretraining and finetuning of BERT. Insufficient capacity and the absence of a clear initialization guide are some of the existing KD method’s area of improvement.

**Parameter Sharing.** Sharing parameters across different layers is a widely used idea for model compression. There have been several attempts to apply parameter sharing in transformer architecture and BERT model. However, the existing parameter sharing methods exhibit a large tradeoff between model performance and model size. They reduce the model’s size significantly but also suffers from a great loss in performance as a result.

## 3 PROPOSED METHODS

In the following, we provide an overview of the main challenges faced in KD and our methods to address them in Section 3.1. We then discuss the precise procedures of SPS and PTP in Sections 3.2 and 3.3. Lastly, we explain our final method, PeaBERT and the training details in Section 3.4.

## 3.1 OVERVIEW

BERT-base model contains over 109 million parameters. Its extensive size makes model deployment often infeasible and computationally expensive in many cases, such as on mobile devices. As a result, industry practitioners commonly use a smaller version of BERT and apply KD. However, the existing KD methods face the following challenges:

1. **Insufficient model capacity of the student model.** Since the student model contains fewer number of parameters than the teacher model, its model capacity is also lower. The smaller and simpler the student model gets, the gap between the student and the teacher grows, making it increasingly difficult for the student to replicate the teacher model’s accuracy. The limited capacity hinders the student model’s performance. How can we enlarge the student model’s capacity while maintaining the same number of parameters?

2. **Absence of proper initial guide for the student model.** There is no widely accepted and vetted guide to selecting the student’s initial state of the KD process. In most cases, a truncated version of pretrained BERT-base model is used. In reality, this hinders the student from reproducing the teacher’s results. Can we find a better method for the student’s KD initialization?

We propose the following main ideas to address these challenges:

1. **Shuffled Parameter Sharing (SPS): increasing ‘effective’ model capacity of the student.** SPS consists of two steps. Step 1 increases the student’s effective model capacity by stacking parameter-shared layers. Step 2 further improves the model capacity by shuffling the shared parameters. Shuffling enables more efficient use of the parameters by learning diverse set of information. As a result, the SPS-applied student model achieves much higher accuracy while still using the same number of parameters (details in Section 3.2).

2. **Pretraining with Teacher’s Predictions (PTP): a novel pretraining task utilizing teacher’s predictions for student initialization.** To address the limitation of the initial guide, we propose PTP, a novel pretraining method for the student by utilizing teacher model’s predictions. Through PTP, the student model pre-learns knowledge latent in the teacher’s softmax output which is hard to learn during the conventional KD process. This helps the student better acquire and utilize the teacher’s knowledge during the KD process (details in Section 3.3).

The following subsections describe the procedures of SPS, PTP, and PeaBERT in detail.

3

Under review as a conference paper at ICLR 2021

- 3.2 SHUFFLED PARAMETER SHARING (SPS)

Q↔K Shared
Layer 2 Layer 2 Q ↔ K Q↔K &
Step 1 Layer 1 Shared Layer 1 Step 2 Q ↔ K Shared& SPS ShuffledLayers
Layer 3 Layer 3 Layers Layer 3 Shuffled
Layers
Layer 2 Layer 2 Layer 2
Layer 1 Layer 1 Layer 1
(a) SPS step 1 (b) SPS step 1 + step 2 (c) SPS for 6-layer stud.
**----- End of picture text -----**
Figure 1: Graphical representation of SPS: (a) first step of SPS (b) second step of SPS, and (c) modified SPS for a 6-layer student.
SPS improves student’s model capacity while using the same number of parameters, addressing the capacity limitations of a typical KD. SPS is composed of the following two steps.

**Step1. Paired Parameter Sharing.** We start with doubling the number of layers in the student model. We then share the parameters between the bottom half and the upper half of the model, as graphically represented in Figure 1a.

**Step2. Shuffling.** We shuffle the Query and Key parameters between the shared pairs. That is, for the shared upper half of layers we use the original Query parameters as Key parameters, and the original Key parameters as Query parameters.
We call this architecture SPS, which is depicted in Figure 1b. For the 6-layer student case we slightly modify the architecture as shown in Figure 1c. We apply SPS on the top 3 layers only.

**Motivation behind step 1: Increased model capacity due to a more complex structure.** By applying step 1, the student model now has more layers while maintaining the same number of parameters used. This leads to a higher model complexity, which we expect will increase model capacity and performance.

**Motivation behind step 2: Increased parameter efficiency and additional regularization effect.** The intuition behind shuffling becomes clear when we compare the model with and without step 2. Let us consider the case depicted in Figure1b. In step 1, the first layer’s Query parameter is used as a Query parameter in both the first layer and in the shared fourth layer. Since this parameter is used only as a Query parameter throughout the model, it will only learn the information relevant to Query. In step 2, on the other hand, this parameter’s function changes due to shuffling. The first layer’s Query parameter is used as a Key parameter in the shared fourth layer. Thus it has the opportunity to learn the important features of both the Query and the Key, gaining a more diverse and wider breadth of knowledge. The shuffling mechanism allows the parameters to learn diverse information, thereby maximizing learning efficiency. We can also view this as a type of regularization or multi-task learning. Since these parameters need to learn the features of both the Query and the Key, this prevents overfitting to either one. Therefore, we expect the shuffling mechanism to improve the generalization ability of the model.

## 3.3 PRETRAINING WITH TEACHER’S PREDICTIONS (PTP)

There can be several candidates for KD-specialized initialization. We propose a pretraining approach called PTP, and experimentally show that it improves KD accuracy.

Most of the previous studies on KD do not elaborate on the initialization of the student model. There are some studies that use a pretrained student model as an initial state, but those pretraining tasks are irrelevant to either the teacher model or the downstream task. To the best of our knowledge, our study is the first case that pretrains the student model with a task relevant to the teacher model and its downstream task. PTP consists of the following two steps.

**Step 1. Creating artificial data based on the teacher’s predictions (PTP labels).**

4

Under review as a conference paper at ICLR 2021

We first input the training data in the teacher model and collect the teacher model’s predictions. We then define ”confidence” as the following. We apply softmax function to the teacher model’s predictions, and the maximum value of the predictions is defined as the confidence. Next, with a specific threshold ”t” (a hyperparameter between 0.5 and 1.0), we assign a new label to the training data according to the rules listed in Table 1. We call these new artificial labels PTP labels.

Table 1: Assigning new PTP labels to the training data.

|Teacher’s prediction correct|confdence_>_t|PTP label|
|---|---|---|
|True|True|confdently correct|
|True|False|unconfdently correct|
|False|True|confdently wrong|
|False|False|unconfdently wrong|

**Step 2. Pretrain the student model to predict the PTP labels.** Using the artificial PTP labels (data _x_ , PTP label) we created, we now pretrain the student model to predict the PTP label when _x_ is provided as an input. In other words, the student model is trained to predict the PTP labels given the downstream training dataset. We train the student model until convergence.
Once these two steps are complete, we use this PTP-pretrained student model as the initial state for the KD process.

**Motivation behind PTP: A relatively easy way of learning the teacher’s generalized knowledge.** The core idea is to make PTP labels by explicitly expressing important information from the teachers’ softmax outputs, such as whether the teacher model predicted correctly or how confident the teacher model is. Pretraining using these labels would help the student acquire the teacher’s generalized knowledge latent in the teacher’s softmax output. This pretraining makes the student model better prepared for the actual KD process. For example, if a teacher makes an incorrect prediction for a data instance x, then we know that data instance x is generally a difficult one to predict. Since this knowledge is only obtainable by directly comparing the true label with the teachers output, it would be difficult for the student to acquire this in the conventional KD process. Representing this type of information through PTP labels and training the student to predict them could help the student acquire deeply latent knowledge included in the teacher’s output much easily. Intuitively, we expect that a student model that has undergone such a pretraining session is better prepared for the actual KD process and will likely achieve better results.

## 3.4 PEABERT: SPS AND PTP COMBINED

## 3.4.1 OVERALL FRAMEWORK OF PEABERT

PeaBERT applies SPS and PTP together on BERT for maximum impact on performance. Given a student model, PeaBERT first transforms it into an SPS model and applies PTP. Once PTP is completed, we use this model as the initial state of the student model for the KD process. The overall framework of PeaBERT is depicted in Figure 2.

## 3.4.2 LEARNING DETAILS OF PEABERT

For the starting point of the KD process, a well-finetuned teacher model should be used. We use the 12 layer BERT-base model as the teacher. The learned parameters are denoted as:

The _θ[t]_ denotes parameters of the teacher, _σ_ denotes the softmax function, _xi_ denotes the training data, _zt_ denotes the teacher model’s output predictions, _yi_ denotes the true labels, and _LCE_ denotes cross-entropy loss.

We then pretrain the student model with PTP labels using the following loss:

The loss function is as follows:

## (5)

where _LKL_ denotes the Kullback-Leibler divergence loss, _K_ denotes the index pairs of layers we use for intermediate layer-wise distillation, and _z[k]_ denotes the output logits of the k-th layer. _α_ and _β_ are hyperparameters.
Note that during the KD process, we use a softmax-temperature T, which controls the softness of teacher model’s output predictions introduced in (Hinton et al. (2015)).

4 EXPERIMENTS

We discuss experimental results to assess the effectiveness of our proposed method. Our goal is to answer the following questions.
- **Q1. Overall performance.** How does PeaBERT perform compared to the currently existing KD methods? (Section 4.2)
- **Q2. Effectiveness of SPS.** To what extent does SPS improve the effective capacity of the student model without increasing the number of parameters? (Section 4.3)

- **Q3. Effectiveness of PTP.** Is PTP a good initialization method? Compared to the conventionally-used truncated version of the BERT-base model, what is the impact of PTP on the model’s performance? (Section 4.4)

## 4.1 EXPERIMENTAL SETTINGS

**Datasets.** We use four of the most widely used datasets in the General Language Understanding Evaluation (GLUE) benchmark (Wang et al. (2018)): SST-2[1] , QNLI[2] , RTE[3] , and MRPC[4] . For sentiment classification, we use the Stanford Sentiment Treebank (SST-2) (Socher et al. (2013)). For

> 1https://nlp.stanford.edu/sentiment/index.html

> 2https://rajpurkar.github.io/SQuAD-explorer/

> 3https://aclweb.org/aclwiki/RecognizingTextualEntailment

> 4https://www.microsoft.com/en-us/download/details.aspx?id=52398

6

Under review as a conference paper at ICLR 2021

Table 2: Overall results of PeaBERT compared to the state-of-the-art KD baseline, PatientKD. The results are evaluated on the test set of GLUE official benchmark. The subscript numbers denote the number of independent layers of the student.

Table 3: PeaBERT in comparison to other state-of-the-art competitors in dev set. The cited results of the competitors are from the official papers of each method. For accurate comparison, model dimensions are fixed to six layers across all models compared.

natural language inference, we use QNLI(Rajpurkar et al. (2016)) and RTE. For paraphrase similarity matching, we use Microsoft Research Paraphrase Corpus (MRPC) (Dolan & Brockett (2005)). Specifically, SST-2 is a movie review dataset with binary annotations where the binary label indicates positive and negative reviews. QNLI is a task for predicting whether a pair of a question and an answer is an entailment or not. RTE is based on a series of textual entailment challenges and MRPC contains pairs of sentences and corresponding labels, where the labels indicate the semantic equivalence relationship between the sentences in each pair.

**Competitors.** We use Patient Knowledge Distillation (PatientKD, Sun et al. (2019)) as our baseline learning method to compare and quantify the effectiveness of PeaBERT. PatientKD is one of the most widely used baselines and is a variant of the original Knowledge Distillation method (Hinton et al. (2006)). We conduct experiments on BERT model (Devlin et al. (2018)) and compare the results of PeaBERT to the original BERT. In addition, we compare the results with other stateof-the-art BERT-distillation models, including DistilBERT(Sanh et al. (2019)) and TinyBERT(Jiao et al. (2019)).

**Training Details.** We use the 12-layer original BERT model (Devlin et al. (2018)) as a teacher model and further finetune the teacher for each task independently. The student models are created using the same architecture as the original BERT, but the number of layers is reduced to either 1, 2, 3, or 6 depending on experiments. That is, we initialize the student model using the first _n_ -layers of parameters from the pretrained original BERT obtained from Google’s official BERT repo[5] . We use the standard baseline PatientKD and the following hyperparameter settings: training batch size from _{_ 32, 64 _}_ , learning rate from _{_ 1, 2, 3, 5 _}·_ 10 _[−]_[5] , number of epochs from _{_ 4, 6, 10 _}_ , _α_ between _{_ 0.1 and 0.7 _}_ , and _β_ between _{_ 0 and 500 _}_ .

## 4.2 OVERALL PERFORMANCE

We summarize the performance of PeaBERT against the standard baseline PatientKD in Table 2. We also compare the results of PeaBERT to the competitors DistilBERT and TinyBERT in Table 3. We observe the following from the results.

First, we see from Table 2 that PeaBERT consistently yields higher performance in downstream tasks across all three model sizes. Notably, PeaBERT shows improved accuracy by an average of 4.7% for the 1-layer student, 5.1% for the 2-layer student, and 3.3% for the 3-layer student (Note that we use F1 score for MRPC when calculating the average accuracy throughout the experiments).

> 5https://github.com/google-research/bert

7

Under review as a conference paper at ICLR 2021

A maximum improvement of 14.8% is achieved in PeaBERT1-QNLI. These results validate the effectiveness of PeaBERT across varying downstream tasks and student model sizes.

Second, using the same number of parameters, PeaBERT outperforms the state-of-the-art KD baselines DistilBERT and TinyBERT, by 5.6% and 1.3% on average. We use a 6-layer student model for this comparison. An advantage of PeaBERT is that it achieves remarkable accuracy improvement just by using the downstream dataset without touching the original pretraining tasks. Unlike its competitors DistilBERT and TinyBERT, PeaBERT does not touch the original pretraining tasks, Masked Language Modeling (MLM) and Next Sentence Prediction (NSP). This reduces training time significantly. For example, DistilBERT took approximately 90 hours with eight 16GB V100 GPUs, while PeaBERT took a minimum of one minute (PeaBERT1 with RTE) to a maximum of one hour (PeaBERT3 with QNLI) using just two NVIDIA T4 GPUs.

Finally, another advantage of PeaBERT is that it can be applied to other transformer-based models with minimal modifications. The SPS method can be directly applied to any transformer-based models, and the PTP method can be applied to any classification task.

## 4.3 EFFECTIVENESS OF SPS

Table 4: An ablation study to validate each step of SPS. The results are derived using GLUE dev set.

We perform ablation studies to verify the effectiveness of SPS. We compare three models BERT3, SPS-1, and SPS-2. BERT3 is the original BERT model with 3 layers, which applies none of the SPS steps. SPS-1 applies only the first SPS step (paired parameter sharing) to BERT3. SPS-2 applies both the first step and the second step (shuffling) to BERT3.

The results are summarized in Table 4. Compared to the original BERT3, SPS-1 shows improved accuracy in all the downstream datasets with an average of 1.1%, verifying our first motivation. Comparing SPS-1 with SPS-2, we note that SPS-2 consistently shows even better performance with an average of 1.9%, which validates our second motivation. Based on these results, we conclude that both steps of the SPS process work as intended to increase the student model’s capacity without increasing the number of parameters used.

## 4.4 EFFECTIVENESS OF PTP

Table 5: An ablation study to verify PTP. The results are derived using GLUE dev set.

We perform an ablation study to validate the effectiveness of using PTP as an initial guide for the student model. We use BERT3+SPS, which is SPS applied on BERT3, as our base model. We compare the results of PTP to its variants PTP-1 and PTP-2. Note that PTP uses four labels constructed from two types of information latent in the teacher model’s softmax prediction: (1) whether the teacher predicted correctly and (2) how confident the teacher is. PTP-1 is a variant of PTP that uses only two labels that state whether the teacher predicts correctly or not. PTP-2 is another PTP variant that uses two labels to state whether the teacher’s predictions is confident or not. PTP-1 and PTP-2 contain different type of teacher’s information in their labels, respectively. From the results summarized in Table 5, we see that PTP-1 and PTP-2 increase the student’s accuracy by 0.5% and 0.8% on average, respectively. Combining PTP-1 and PTP-2, which is essentially our proposed PTP method, further improves model accuracy by an average of 1.5%. This study supports the efficacy of our PTP method.

8

Under review as a conference paper at ICLR 2021

These results prove two things: (1) The student that goes through only the conventional KD process does not fully utilize the knowledge included in the teacher models softmax outputs, and (2) PTP does help the student better utilize the knowledge included in teachers softmax outputs. This firmly supports the efficacy of our PTP method and also validates our main claim that initializing a student model with KD-specialized method prior to applying KD can improve accuracy. As existing KD methods do not place much emphasis on the initialization process, this finding highlights a potentially major, undiscovered path to improving model accuracy. Further and deeper researches related to KD-specialized initialization could be promising.

## 5 CONCLUSION

In this paper, we propose Pea-KD, a new KD method for transformer-based distillation, and show its efficacy. Our goal is to address and reduce the limitations of the currently available KD methods: insufficient model capacity and absence of proper initial guide for the student. We first introduce SPS, a new parameter sharing approach that uses a shuffling mechanism, which enhances the capacity of the student model while using the same number of parameters. We then introduce PTP, a KD-specific initialization method for the student model. Our proposed PeaBERT comes from applying these two methods SPS and PTP on BERT. Through extensive experiments conducted using multiple datasets and varying model sizes, we show that our method improves KD accuracy by an average of 4.4% on the GLUE test set. We also show that PeaBERT works well across different datasets, and outperforms the original BERT as well as other state-of-the-art baselines on BERT distillation by an average of 3.5%.

As a future work, we plan to delve deeper into the concept of KD-specialized initialization of the student model. Also, since PTP and SPS are independent processes on their own, we plan to combine PTP and SPS with other model compression techniques, such as weight pruning and quantization.

- Zhilin Yang, Zihang Dai, Yiming Yang, Jaime Carbonell, Russ R Salakhutdinov, and Quoc V Le. Xlnet: Generalized autoregressive pretraining for language understanding. In _Advances in neural information processing systems_ , pp. 5753–5763, 2019.

This document was extracted by OCR engine and could contain mistakes.

zero_shot__gemini-3-flash-preview.label: Zero Shot (gemini-3-flash-preview)
zero_shot__gemini-3-flash-preview.overall_feedback: The paper presents an interesting approach to knowledge distillation (KD) by combining Shuffled Parameter Sharing (SPS) and a novel initialization strategy (PTP). The empirical results on GLUE are strong, particularly regarding training efficiency compared to models like TinyBERT. However, the paper suffers from some lack of clarity in Equation 4, where the PTP loss function is mentioned but the corresponding soft-target formula is omitted, and some potential confusion in Table 2 regarding parameter counts for the baseline models. The technical intuition for 'SPS Step 2' (shuffling Q and K) is creative, though the theoretical justification is somewhat hand-wavy.

comments.title: Unspecified PTP Loss Formulation
comments.quote: \mathcal{L}_{PTP} = \sum_{i} L_{CE}(\sigma(z_s(x_i)), y_i^{[PTP]})
comments.explanation: Equation 4 defines the PTP loss as a cross-entropy loss between student predictions and PTP labels. However, Table 1 describes four labels ('confidently correct', etc.). It is not explicitly stated whether the student uses a standard 4-way classification head for this pretraining task or how this head is discarded/integrated when transitioning to the downstream task distillation. While a reader can infer a task-specific head is used, the transition between θ_PTP and the final KD initial state is a bit vague.

comments.title: Ambiguity in Baseline Parameter Counts
comments.quote: Table 2: ... The subscript numbers denote the number of independent layers of the student.
comments.explanation: In Table 2, the authors compare 'PeaBERT1' (etc.) against 'BERT1-PatientKD'. In Section 3.2, the authors explain that PeaBERT 'doubles the number of layers' while sharing parameters. If PeaBERT1 has 1 'independent' layer but is stacked into 2 layers, and the baseline 'BERT1' is a standard 1-layer model, the performance gain might be attributed to the increased depth (2 layers) rather than just the distillation method. The text 'doubling the number of layers' suggests PeaBERT1 actually has 2 physical layers, which makes the comparison against a single-layer baseline slightly mismatched in terms of architectural depth, even if parameter counts are identical.

comments.title: Potential Index Error in Loss Function
comments.quote: \mathcal{L}_{KD} = \sum_{i} [ \alpha L_{CE}(\sigma(z_s), y_i) + (1-\alpha) L_{KL}(\sigma(z_s), \sigma(z_t)) ] + \beta \sum_{k \in K} MSE(z_s^{[k]}, z_t^{[k]})
comments.explanation: In Equation 5, the index 'i' (summing over training samples) is used for the CE and KL terms, but it is missing from the summation for the MSE term. Furthermore, the intermediate MSE loss typically requires a projection matrix when layer dimensions differ, or a specific mapping between student layer k and teacher layer g(k). While the text mentions 'index pairs', the formal notation in the equation lacks the sample index 'i', implying a sum over layers but not over the dataset for that specific term.

comments.title: Questionable Claim regarding Effective Capacity
comments.quote: Doing so increases the model’s effective capacity which enables the student to better replicate the teacher model
comments.explanation: The authors claim that stacking shared layers (Step 1) and shuffling (Step 2) increases 'effective capacity'. While depth generally increases receptivity, parameter sharing is often viewed as a constraint that reduces the hypothesis space. The claim that shuffling Q and K 'maximizes learning efficiency' because parameters learn 'both features' is an interesting intuition but lacks a formal basis. It is more likely acting as a structural prior than an increase in raw capacity. The term 'effective capacity' is used loosely here.

comments.title: Inconsistency in PTP Label Definition
comments.quote: Table 1: ... confidence > t ... PTP label
comments.explanation: The paper defines the threshold 't' as a hyperparameter between 0.5 and 1.0. For a teacher in a binary classification task (like MRPC or SST-2), the confidence (max of softmax) is naturally between 0.5 and 1.0. However, for multi-class tasks with many categories (though GLUE tasks here are mostly binary or ternary), the max probability could easily be below 0.5. The authors don't clarify how the threshold 't' adapts to tasks where the teacher's 'confident' prediction might be numerically lower than 0.5.

zero_shot__qwen3-235b-a22b-2507.label: Zero Shot (qwen3-235b-a22b-2507)
zero_shot__qwen3-235b-a22b-2507.overall_feedback: The paper presents a novel and well-motivated approach to knowledge distillation—Pea-KD—with two complementary components: Shuffled Parameter Sharing (SPS) and Pretraining with Teacher’s Predictions (PTP). The ideas are conceptually clear and address meaningful limitations in existing KD methods. The experimental evaluation is thorough, with ablation studies and comparisons across multiple datasets and baselines. The results are impressive, especially the gains over strong baselines like TinyBERT. However, there are several technical and logical issues in the formulation and presentation that raise concerns about reproducibility and correctness, particularly around the mismatch between the described loss functions and training procedure. Some claims also appear overstated relative to what is actually demonstrated.

comments.title: Incorrect KL divergence loss usage in equation (5)
comments.quote:
The loss function is as follows: 

## (5) 

where _LKL_ denotes the Kullback-Leibler divergence loss, _K_ denotes the index pairs of layers we use for intermediate layer-wise distillation, and _z[k]_ denotes the output logits of the k-th layer. _α_ and _β_ are hyperparameters.
comments.explanation: The KL divergence in standard knowledge distillation is typically computed between soft labels from the teacher and student, using a temperature-scaled softmax. However, the description of equation (5) suggests that _LKL_ is applied to final predictions (logits), and the text mentions using temperature T—but it does not clarify whether the KL divergence is properly computed with softened probabilities. Moreover, the equation itself is missing from the OCR output, so the actual form of the loss is ambiguous. Even assuming the equation is present in the original, the text does not specify that the KL term uses temperature scaling, which is critical for correctness. This omission could mislead readers about the implementation. Given that KL divergence with temperature is standard (Hinton et al. 2015), the lack of clarity here is a moderate issue for reproducibility.

comments.title: Mismatch between described PTP loss and task
comments.quote:
We then pretrain the student model with PTP labels using the following loss: 

where _yi[PT P]_ denotes the PTP labels and the subscript _s_ denotes the student model.
comments.explanation: The paper states that PTP involves predicting one of four categorical labels (confidently correct, unconfidently correct, etc.), so the loss should be a standard cross-entropy for a 4-class classification task. However, the loss function is not shown, and the subscript notation _yi[PT P]_ is ambiguous—particularly due to OCR artifacts like "[PT P]". More importantly, the model architecture (a transformer encoder) is designed for downstream tasks like binary classification (e.g., SST-2), but PTP introduces a 4-class auxiliary task. The paper does not explain how the final layer is adapted (e.g., a 4-way classifier head) or whether this head is discarded or reused during KD. This is a critical design detail for reproducibility. While the intent may be clear to experts, the omission constitutes an underspecification of the training pipeline.

comments.title: Overstatement of performance improvement over original BERT
comments.quote: When tested on four widely used GLUE tasks, PeaBERT improves student’s accuracy by 4.4% on average and up to 14.8% maximum when compared to the original BERT model.
comments.explanation: This claim is misleading. Table 2 clearly shows that all PeaBERT variants (except possibly PeaBERT3 on MRPC) perform below BERT-base. For example, PeaBERT3 averages 81.7 vs. BERT-base’s 84.8. The 4.4% improvement is likely intended to be relative to baseline KD methods (e.g., BERT3-PatientKD), not the full BERT. The phrase "compared to the original BERT model" is therefore incorrect and overstates the result. Even if this is a typo, the prose in both the abstract and contributions section repeats this erroneous comparison. This misrepresents the contribution and is a major issue in interpretability.

comments.title: Underspecified shuffling operation in SPS
comments.quote: We shuffle the Query and Key parameters between the shared pairs. That is, for the shared upper half of layers we use the original Query parameters as Key parameters, and the original Key parameters as Query parameters.
comments.explanation: The description of shuffling is ambiguous. It states that in the upper half, original Query parameters are used as Key and Key as Query. But parameters are tied between lower and upper layers due to sharing. So if layer 1 and layer 4 share parameters, and layer 4 now uses Q as K, does that mean the same parameter matrix is used as Q in layer 1 and as K in layer 4? If so, is this a reinterpretation at inference, or a structural change? The paper lacks a mathematical definition of how the attention computation is modified. Without this, the mechanism is underspecified. While Figure 1 provides intuition, the exact forward pass—especially how Q and K are swapped without breaking symmetry—is not clear. This affects reproducibility and constitutes a moderate issue.

comments.title: Inconsistency in average improvement claims
comments.quote: PeaBERT improves student’s accuracy by 4.4% on average in four GLUE tasks... improves the student model’s performance by 4.4% on average
comments.explanation: The paper states a 4.4% average improvement multiple times, but Table 2 shows larger gains: 4.7% (1-layer), 5.1% (2-layer), and 3.3% (3-layer) averages over PatientKD. Averaging these gives ~4.4%, which suggests the number is a rough aggregate. However, in Section 4.2, it says "PeaBERT shows improved accuracy by an average of 4.7% for the 1-layer student...", which contradicts the earlier claim that the overall improvement is 4.4%. While 4.4% may be a rounded aggregate, the lack of clarity on what exactly is being averaged (across models? across tasks?) and the inconsistent presentation across abstract and main text introduces ambiguity. This is a minor framing issue, as the data supports a strong gain, but the inconsistency is noticeable.

comments.title: Missing definition of intermediate distillation loss in equation (5)
comments.quote:
a mean-squared error loss between the intermediate layers of student and teacher models. 

## (5) 

where _LKL_ denotes the Kullback-Leibler divergence loss, _K_ denotes the index pairs of layers we use for intermediate layer-wise distillation, and _z[k]_ denotes the output logits of the k-th layer.
comments.explanation: The text says the loss includes an MSE term between intermediate layers, but in the description of equation (5), only _LKL_ is mentioned alongside _K_ and _z[k]_, and no MSE term appears. The notation _K_ is said to denote "index pairs of layers", but it's unclear how this fits into a KL or MSE loss. If _K_ indexes pairs, is the MSE computed between corresponding layers? The equation is missing due to OCR, but the text fails to clarify the composite structure of the loss. A standard formulation would have separate terms for final output KL and intermediate MSE (e.g., layer activations). The current description blurs these, risking confusion. This is a moderate technical issue, especially since layer-wise distillation is a core component.

zero_shot__glm-4.6.label: Zero Shot (glm-4.6)
zero_shot__glm-4.6.overall_feedback: The paper presents an interesting approach to knowledge distillation with two novel components: SPS for parameter-efficient capacity increase and PTP for teacher-aware initialization. The experimental results show significant improvements over baselines. However, the paper suffers from several underspecified methodological details that hinder reproducibility and full understanding of the approach. The core ideas appear sound, but key implementation details are missing, particularly regarding hyperparameter settings, architectural specifics, and training procedures.

comments.title: Missing hyperparameter values
comments.quote: We use the standard baseline PatientKD and the following hyperparameter settings: training batch size from {32, 64}, learning rate from {1, 2, 3, 5}·10[−]5, number of epochs from {4, 6, 10}, α between {0.1 and 0.7}, and β between {0 and 500}.
comments.explanation: The paper lists ranges for all important hyperparameters but never specifies which values were actually used in the experiments. This makes it impossible to reproduce the reported results. The authors should have reported the specific hyperparameter values used for each experiment, or at least the values used for the main results in Tables 2 and 3.

comments.title: SPS parameter sharing not fully specified
comments.quote: We start with doubling the number of layers in the student model. We then share the parameters between the bottom half and the upper half of the model... We shuffle the Query and Key parameters between the shared pairs.
comments.explanation: The description of SPS is ambiguous about which parameters are shared. Are all transformer parameters (attention weights, feed-forward weights, layer norms) shared between layers, or only the Query/Key matrices? The text mentions shuffling Query and Key parameters, implying these are shared, but doesn't clarify about Value matrices or feed-forward network parameters. This ambiguity makes it difficult to understand and reproduce the SPS architecture.

comments.title: PTP training procedure underspecified
comments.quote: Using the artificial PTP labels (data x, PTP label) we created, we now pretrain the student model to predict the PTP label when x is provided as an input. We train the student model until convergence.
comments.explanation: The paper doesn't specify important training details for PTP: how many epochs, what learning rate, batch size, or optimizer were used? 'Until convergence' is vague without specifying convergence criteria. Also, it's unclear whether PTP uses all training data or a subset, and what the class distribution is among the four PTP labels. These details are crucial for reproducing the PTP component.

comments.title: PTP threshold value not specified
comments.quote: Next, with a specific threshold 't' (a hyperparameter between 0.5 and 1.0), we assign a new label to the training data according to the rules listed in Table 1.
comments.explanation: The paper mentions that t is a hyperparameter between 0.5 and 1.0 but never reports what value was used in the experiments. Since the threshold directly affects the PTP label distribution and thus the pretraining objective, this is a critical missing detail for reproducibility.

comments.title: Temperature T value not specified
comments.quote: Note that during the KD process, we use a softmax-temperature T, which controls the softness of teacher model's output predictions introduced in (Hinton et al. (2015)).
comments.explanation: The paper mentions using a temperature T in the KL divergence term of the loss function but never specifies what value was used. Temperature is a critical hyperparameter in knowledge distillation that significantly affects results, so this omission hinders reproducibility.

comments.title: Layer pairs for intermediate distillation not specified
comments.quote: where L_KL denotes the Kullback-Leibler divergence loss, K denotes the index pairs of layers we use for intermediate layer-wise distillation, and z[k] denotes the output logits of the k-th layer.
comments.explanation: The paper mentions using intermediate layer distillation with layer pairs indexed by K, but never specifies which layers were paired. For example, were layers (1,2) paired with (6,7), or some other mapping? This detail is important for understanding how knowledge is transferred between intermediate layers.

comments.title: SPS modification for 6-layer student not explained
comments.quote: For the 6-layer student case we slightly modify the architecture as shown in Figure 1c. We apply SPS on the top 3 layers only.
comments.explanation: The paper states that SPS is modified for 6-layer students but doesn't explain why this modification is necessary or how exactly it works. Why apply SPS only to the top 3 layers? What happens to the bottom 3 layers? This lack of explanation makes it difficult to understand the design choice and its implications.

comments.title: Computational complexity not analyzed
comments.quote: As a result, the SPS-applied student model achieves much higher accuracy while still using the same number of parameters
comments.explanation: While SPS maintains parameter count, it doubles the number of layers and adds parameter sharing/shuffling overhead. The paper doesn't analyze or report the computational cost (e.g., inference time, FLOPs) of SPS compared to a standard student model. This is an important practical consideration for model deployment that's overlooked.

zero_shot__gemini-3.1-flash-lite-preview.label: Zero Shot (gemini-3.1-flash-lite-preview)
zero_shot__gemini-3.1-flash-lite-preview.overall_feedback: This paper presents a novel approach to Knowledge Distillation (KD) for transformer-based models by combining a parameter-sharing technique (SPS) and an initialization strategy (PTP). The methodology is intuitive and well-motivated by the limitations of conventional KD. However, the paper suffers from significant omissions in formal notation, specifically regarding the definition of objective functions and parameters. In several instances, the text refers to mathematical expressions or variables that are not explicitly defined or indexed, requiring the reader to guess the intended formalization. While the experimental results demonstrate consistent gains, the lack of rigorous definitions hinders the reproducibility and formal clarity required for an academic contribution.

comments.title: Missing formal definitions for loss functions
comments.quote:
The learned parameters are denoted as: 


The $\theta[t]$ denotes parameters of the teacher, $\sigma$ denotes the softmax function, $xi$ denotes the training data, $zt$ denotes the teacher model’s output predictions, $yi$ denotes the true labels, and $LCE$ denotes cross-entropy loss.

We then pretrain the student model with PTP labels using the following loss:


where $yi[PT P]$ denotes the PTP labels and the subscript $s$ denotes the student model.
comments.explanation: The paper sets up the formal framework for the loss functions but fails to display the actual equations. Both the initial KD loss definition (referenced as part of the learned parameters section) and the PTP loss function (referenced after the description of pretraining) are completely missing from the text. The placeholders for these equations are empty, making it impossible to verify the exact formulation used in the training process.

comments.title: Ambiguity in loss function indices
comments.quote: where $LKL$ denotes the Kullback-Leibler divergence loss, $K$ denotes the index pairs of layers we use for intermediate layer-wise distillation, and $z[k]$ denotes the output logits of the k-th layer.
comments.explanation: Equation (5) is referred to but not explicitly provided in the text. Furthermore, the description of the loss function mentions 'index pairs of layers' ($K$), but the notation $z[k]$ is defined as logits of the k-th layer. Standard layer-wise distillation (such as in FitNets or Patient KD) usually involves mapping/regressing one layer to another; the current prose is underspecified as to whether $K$ refers to a mapping or simply a subset of layers being distilled, and provides no mathematical expression to clarify how these are computed.

comments.title: Under-specified PTP thresholding and label construction
comments.quote: Next, with a specific threshold ”t” (a hyperparameter between 0.5 and 1.0), we assign a new label to the training data according to the rules listed in Table 1.
comments.explanation: While Table 1 provides categories, the actual conversion from the teacher's softmax output to the 'confident/unconfident' labels is not mathematically defined. While it is implied that 'confidence' is the max value of the softmax, the mapping process—specifically how the threshold $t$ is applied in conjunction with the 'correctness' criteria to form the final labels—is described in general terms rather than as a formal algorithm. This leaves room for ambiguity regarding how the four categories (confidently correct, unconfidently correct, etc.) are effectively handled in the cross-entropy loss.

zero_shot__deepseek-v4-flash.label: Zero Shot (deepseek-v4-flash)
zero_shot__deepseek-v4-flash.overall_feedback: The paper proposes Pea-KD, a knowledge distillation method combining shuffled parameter sharing (SPS) and pretraining with teacher predictions (PTP). The core ideas are interesting and the empirical results show improvements over baselines. However, the paper suffers from several clarity and consistency issues that undermine reproducibility. The most critical is a mismatch between the described SPS mechanism and the reported parameter counts, especially for the 6-layer student model in Table 3. Additionally, the loss function is not fully specified, and the application of SPS to different student sizes is ambiguous. These issues need to be resolved before the paper can be considered for acceptance.

comments.title: Inconsistent parameter count for 6-layer PeaBERT
comments.quote: For the 6-layer student case we slightly modify the architecture as shown in Figure 1c. We apply SPS on the top 3 layers only." and from Table 3: "# of parameters ... 42.6M" for all methods including PeaBERT.
comments.explanation: The paper claims that PeaBERT for the 6-layer student uses the same number of parameters (42.6M) as DistilBERT and TinyBERT. However, applying SPS as described (doubling layers with parameter sharing, or sharing only top 3 layers) would result in far fewer independent parameters. For a 6-layer student with SPS applied only to top 3 layers (sharing with bottom 3), the effective number of independent layers is 3, yielding roughly half the parameters of a full 6-layer model. The paper does not explain how 42.6M is reached, nor does it clarify the actual architecture (hidden size, number of layers, etc.). This is a major inconsistency that makes the comparison unreliable.

comments.title: Missing loss function definition
comments.quote: The loss function is as follows: ## (5)
comments.explanation: Equation (5) is not shown in the provided text. It is only referenced as a placeholder. Without the explicit loss function, the training procedure is underspecified. The surrounding text mentions three loss terms (CE, KL, MSE) and hyperparameters α, β, but the exact combination and any additional details (e.g., temperature scaling) are missing. This prevents reproduction of the experiments.

comments.title: Ambiguity in SPS application for different student sizes
comments.quote: We start with doubling the number of layers in the student model." and "For the 6-layer student case we slightly modify the architecture as shown in Figure 1c. We apply SPS on the top 3 layers only.
comments.explanation: The general description of SPS implies that for any student model, the number of layers is doubled. But for the 6-layer student, the paper says SPS is applied only to the top 3 layers, which contradicts the doubling rule. It is unclear whether the 1-, 2-, and 3-layer students also deviate from the standard SPS or if only the 6-layer case is special. This confuses the architecture for each experimental setting.

comments.title: Unclear initialization of student model for baselines
comments.quote: That is, we initialize the student model using the first n-layers of parameters from the pretrained original BERT obtained from Google’s official BERT repo.
comments.explanation: The paper states that the student is initialized with the first n layers of BERT-base. However, for the SPS models, it is not specified whether the initial parameters are also taken from the first n layers and then SPS is applied, or if the SPS model is initialized differently. This is especially important for the PTP pretraining step, where the student might start from scratch or from the truncated BERT. The ablation studies compare models with and without SPS/PTP, but the initialization commonality is not explicitly stated.

comments.title: Potential overclaim in generality
comments.quote: Our proposed method Pea-KD can be applied to any transformer-based models
comments.explanation: While SPS can be applied to any transformer model in principle, PTP is designed for classification tasks (using teacher predictions to create artificial labels). The paper only demonstrates on GLUE classification tasks. Claiming applicability to any transformer-based model (including generation tasks, regression, etc.) without discussion or experiments is an overstatement. The paper should qualify that PTP is task-specific.

comments.title: Ambiguous notation for PTP-trained parameters
comments.quote: When PTP is completed, we use the θ̂[PT P][s] as the initial state of the KD process.
comments.explanation: The notation uses a hat with subscript and superscript that is difficult to parse due to OCR. It likely means θ̂_{s}^{PTP} or similar. However, the hat is not defined in the paper. It is unclear whether it denotes an estimate, a specific training step, or something else. This could confuse readers.

zero_shot__glm-4.7-flash.label: Zero Shot (glm-4.7-flash)
zero_shot__glm-4.7-flash.overall_feedback: The paper presents a novel KD method for BERT involving Shuffled Parameter Sharing (SPS) and Pretraining with Teacher's Predictions (PTP). The method demonstrates strong empirical results, outperforming baselines like PatientKD and DistilBERT. However, the abstract contains a numerical inconsistency where the average improvement is stated as 3.5%, which matches the average improvement over DistilBERT and TinyBERT rather than PatientKD, the primary comparison in the subsequent analysis. Additionally, the notation for teacher parameters switches from superscripts to brackets, which may cause confusion.

zero_shot__qwen3.6-35b-a3b.label: Zero Shot (qwen3.6-35b-a3b)
zero_shot__qwen3.6-35b-a3b.overall_feedback: The paper proposes a parameter-efficient knowledge distillation framework (Pea-KD) combining shuffled parameter sharing (SPS) and teacher-prediction pretraining (PTP). The core ideas are clearly motivated, and the experimental results demonstrate consistent improvements over strong baselines like PatientKD, DistilBERT, and TinyBERT. However, the manuscript suffers from critical gaps in its mathematical formalization: the loss functions for both the PTP pretraining stage and the final KD stage are entirely absent, likely due to PDF extraction failures. Additionally, the SPS shuffling mechanism is described with enough ambiguity that the exact parameter mappings across the Transformer blocks cannot be fully reconstructed. These issues, combined with a few minor factual typos, need to be addressed before the methodology can be fully validated or reproduced.

comments.title: Missing mathematical formulations for PTP and KD loss functions
comments.quote:
We then pretrain the student model with PTP labels using the following loss:


**----- Start of picture text -----**

True
Label
Teacher Predictions PTP  Teacher Predictions
Labels
Training  Training  Transformer
Data CE  Data Distillation
Loss
PTP-trained CE  True
SPS Student Predictions SPS Student Predictions Loss Label
Student

**----- End of picture text -----**

Figure 2: Overall framework of PeaBERT.
comments.explanation: Initially, I was confused by the abrupt interruption in Section 3.4.2 where the text introduces the PTP loss but immediately follows with figure captions and pipeline diagrams instead of an equation. I checked the surrounding sections and the referenced Figure 2, but these only illustrate data flow, not the mathematical objective. The text never actually defines how the artificial PTP labels are used in a loss function (e.g., standard cross-entropy or a modified variant). This is a critical gap because it leaves the pretraining procedure mathematically undefined and unreproducible.

comments.title: Absence of the KD training loss equation
comments.quote:
The loss function is as follows:


## (5)
comments.explanation: My attention was drawn to this placeholder in Section 3.4.2. The authors explicitly state that the KD process uses three loss terms (cross-entropy, KL divergence, and MSE for intermediate layers) and mention hyperparameters α, β, and temperature T. However, the actual combined loss formula is completely missing, replaced only by '(5)'. Without seeing how these terms are weighted and scaled, it is impossible to verify the contribution of each component or reproduce the distillation process. This omission directly undermines the reproducibility of the core methodology.

comments.title: Underspecified parameter mapping in SPS shuffling
comments.quote: Step2. Shuffling. We shuffle the Query and Key parameters between the shared pairs. That is, for the shared upper half of layers we use the original Query parameters as Key parameters, and the original Key parameters as Query parameters.
comments.explanation: I initially found the intuition behind shuffling Q and K to be sound and well-motivated. However, upon closer inspection, the exact operational procedure is underspecified. It is unclear whether this describes a bidirectional swap ($Q_l \leftrightarrow K_{l'}$) or a one-way substitution ($K_{l'} \leftarrow Q_l$). More importantly, the text omits how the Value (V) and Output (O) projection matrices are handled during this step. In Transformer architectures, these projections are intrinsically tied to Q and K; if they remain unmodified, the shuffling could break standard attention geometric assumptions or gradient flow. Without explicit mapping rules for all four projection matrices, the architectural modification cannot be precisely implemented.

comments.title: Factual typo in BERT parameter count
comments.quote: BERT-base model contains over 109 million parameters.
comments.explanation: I initially questioned whether this referred to a specific subvariant, but cross-referencing standard BERT specifications confirms it has approximately 110 million parameters. '109 million' is clearly a typo, likely intended to be $1.1 \times 10^8$ or '110 million'. Reading it as $10^9$ would incorrectly describe a billion-parameter model. While this does not affect the experimental methodology or results, it introduces an avoidable factual inaccuracy in the model description that a careful reader might initially find confusing.

comments.title: Incomplete notation setup for teacher forward pass
comments.quote:
The learned parameters are denoted as: 


The _θ[t]_ denotes parameters of the teacher, _σ_ denotes the softmax function, _xi_ denotes the training data, _zt_ denotes the teacher model’s output predictions
comments.explanation: I was expecting a formal equation immediately following 'denoted as:' to establish the teacher's forward pass (e.g., $z_t = f_\theta(x_i)$). Instead, the text jumps directly to variable definitions. I checked the context to see if the equation appeared elsewhere, but it is entirely missing. While the variable names are eventually defined, the lack of a baseline forward-pass equation makes the mathematical setup feel disjointed and forces the reader to mentally reconstruct standard notation, which slightly weakens the formal rigor of the methodology section.
